text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Serena Patel
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Serena Patel
Consultant Neurologist,  

Daniel Morris, DOB: 14-03-2003, Hospital No: KCH482917 NHS No. 9432081576  
Flat 3B, 27 Crescent Road, London, SE15 2HT

Thank you for your referral. I met with the patient today in clinic. The history is chiefly patient-reported as no witness accounts were available. He is a university student in the lead-up to examinations and has been studying late into the night with irregular sleep and increased caffeine intake. He described prior episodes over the past year characterised by a brief prodrome of vague unease followed by loss of awareness with subsequent confusion for 20–30 minutes, and occasional tongue biting noted after the event. He reports that these episodes tended to cluster during periods of sleep deprivation. He has kept a simple diary on his phone. According to his own record, there have been no additional events of this nature since early August, despite intermittent late study sessions; in his words, things have been quiet on the seizure front. 

There is no clear precipitating illness or head injury. He denies recreational drug use and drinks alcohol only occasionally; he notes that after one evening of heavier alcohol intake earlier in the year he felt particularly foggy the next morning, though he did not identify a clear event then. He is not driving. Family history is non-contributory by his account. He has noticed brief moments of loss of train of thought during long revision periods, but these are not accompanied by automatisms or witnessed behavioural change and may be related to fatigue and anxiety. 

On examination today he was alert and oriented with normal speech and language. Cranial nerves, motor examination, coordination and gait were within normal limits. There were no focal neurological deficits. Cardiovascular and general examination were unremarkable. 

We discussed the uncertainty around his events given the absence of a reliable eyewitness account and the current gap since the last episode. He is understandably concerned about the impact on his studies. I have advised careful attention to sleep hygiene, moderation of caffeine, and consistent meal routines. He will continue to maintain a contemporaneous record of any further events, including timing, potential triggers, and recovery features. I have requested previous ED notes and any prior investigations he mentioned but which were not available today. Pending review of those records, we will plan outpatient EEG and MRI brain using an epilepsy protocol. He is comfortable with this plan and prefers to avoid any changes to current routines before exams beyond the pragmatic measures above. 

Safety advice was reviewed in clinic, including avoiding unsupervised swimming and working at heights. He confirmed that he is not driving and is aware of the need to seek advice should there be any further events. 

I will update you once investigations are completed or sooner if there is any change. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: According to his own record, there have been no additional events of this nature since early August, despite intermittent late study sessions; in his words, things have been quiet on the seizure front.

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter describes prior episodes over the past year with clustering during sleep deprivation. It states: “According to his own record, there have been no additional events of this nature since early August… in his words, things have been quiet on the seizure front.” Clinic date is 02 October 2025. The most recent confirmed seizure was in early August 2025. Time since last event ≈ from early August to 02 October 2025, which is about 2 months. This does not meet the ≥6 months criterion for ‘seizure free’ definitive label. Specific numeric frequency over a standard period (per day/week/month/year) is not provided. Therefore, the appropriate normalized label is ‘seizure free for multiple months.’
seizure_frequency_number: According to his own record, there have been no additional events of this nature since early August, despite intermittent late study sessions; in his words, things have been quiet on the seizure front.
